title: Background Radiation, Dose Units, and Radiation vs. Contamination
narrationScript: Before you can measure radiation from a source, you have to know what the world reads without one. Every person on Earth receives natural background radiation — radon gas indoors, cosmic rays from space, gamma rays from soil and concrete, and radioactive potassium inside the body. For the average American that totals about three point one millisieverts a year, and radon supplies most of it. Your survey meter always reads a little background, but the regulations exclude it from your occupational dose record. To compare doses at all, you need units. The gray measures energy absorbed per kilogram; the sievert weights that energy for biological harm. One sievert equals one hundred rem, so the five rem occupational limit is fifty millisieverts. Finally, keep two words apart forever. Radiation is energy in flight: it stops when the source is shielded, and it never makes you radioactive. Contamination is radioactive material in the wrong place, delivering dose until it is removed. Sealed sources and six-month leak tests exist to keep contamination out of your job.

sections.heading: The Dose Everyone Already Receives
sections.body:
Stand in an empty field with a sensitive instrument — no licensed source within miles — and it still counts. That is **natural background radiation**, and every human being who has ever lived has been bathed in it. It arrives from four directions at once:

- **Radon and thoron** — radioactive gases that seep out of uranium- and thorium-bearing soil and rock and collect indoors. Their airborne decay products are inhaled and irradiate the lung. At about **2.28 mSv per year**, radon is by far the largest natural contributor for the average American.
- **Cosmic radiation** — high-energy particles from the sun and deep space, filtered by the atmosphere: about **0.33 mSv per year**, climbing with altitude. Denver residents receive roughly twice the cosmic dose of sea-level dwellers, and a coast-to-coast flight adds a few hundredths of a millisievert.
- **Terrestrial gamma** — uranium, thorium, and potassium-40 in soil, rock, brick, and concrete shine on everyone: about **0.21 mSv per year**.
- **Internal emitters** — potassium-40 and carbon-14 built into your own tissues add about **0.29 mSv per year** from the inside. You are, mildly, a radiation source.

Per **NCRP Report No. 160**, natural background totals about **3.1 mSv (310 mrem) per year** for the average American — and medical procedures add roughly as much again for the population as a whole. Worldwide, the natural average is about 2.4 mSv, but geology rules: monazite-sand regions of India and Brazil run many times higher.

sections.caption: Average annual natural background dose in the United States per NCRP Report No. 160 — radon dominates, and the ~3.1 mSv total sits far below the 50 mSv (5 rem) occupational limit.

sections.heading: Background on the Survey Meter — and in the Dose Records
sections.body:
Background has two practical consequences on every radiography job.

**First, your meter reads it.** A working survey meter in an ordinary location reads roughly **0.005 to 0.02 mR/h** of background — a needle that stirs just off zero. That flicker is your daily proof the instrument is alive: a meter reading flat zero is not telling you the area is clean; it is telling you the meter is dead. Professional habit: note the site's background reading **before** the first exposure, because every survey that night — the restricted-area boundary, the device after cranking in — is interpreted *above* that baseline.

**Second, the regulations exclude it.** Under the 10 CFR 20.1003 definitions, **occupational dose does not include background radiation** or medical doses you receive as a patient. The **5 rem (50 mSv)** annual limit is a budget for dose from licensed activity only — the radon in your basement is not charged against it, and Denver's altitude is no excuse when a badge comes back high. Dosimetry processors ship badges with **control badges** for exactly this reason: the control rides along unworn, records background accumulated in transit and storage, and its reading is subtracted so your record reflects occupational dose alone.

sections.heading: One Ladder of Units, Two Languages
sections.body:
Radiation measurement climbs a ladder of three questions — how much ionization in air, how much energy in material, how much harm in tissue — and a U.S. radiographer must speak both the traditional units and the SI units used by the rest of the world.

- **Exposure — roentgen (R).** Ionization produced in *air* by X and gamma photons only (2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg). It is what most U.S. survey meters display, in mR/h.
- **Absorbed dose — rad and gray (Gy).** Energy actually deposited per kilogram of material: **1 Gy = 1 J/kg = 100 rad**.
- **Dose equivalent — rem and sievert (Sv).** Absorbed dose multiplied by a **quality factor** for biological effectiveness: **1** for X, gamma, and beta; **10** for neutrons of unknown energy; **20** for internally deposited alpha (10 CFR 20.1004). **1 Sv = 100 rem.**

For the photons of industrial radiography the quality factor is 1, so the field shorthand holds: **1 R of exposure ≈ 1 rad absorbed ≈ 1 rem dose equivalent** in soft tissue — which is why a 2 mR/h survey reading is treated as 2 mrem/h.

The **sievert** is the primary unit in CP-105 and in most of the world's regulations. The NRC still writes its rules in rem with SI in parentheses: the occupational limit of **5 rem per year is 0.05 Sv = 50 mSv**; the public limit of **0.1 rem per year is 1 mSv**. Same physics, two dialects — and an IRRSP candidate converts between them without hesitation.

sections.caption: The four working quantities of radiation measurement — traditional and SI.
rows: How much ionization in air? (X and gamma only)
How much energy deposited per kilogram of material?
How much biological harm? (absorbed dose × quality factor)
How much radioactive material? (decays per second)

sections.title: Conversions you must own
sections.body:
**1 Sv = 100 rem**, therefore **1 mSv = 100 mrem** and **1 mrem = 10 µSv**.

- Occupational annual TEDE limit: **5 rem = 0.05 Sv = 50 mSv**
- Public annual limit: **0.1 rem = 100 mrem = 1 mSv**
- A 2 mR/h gamma field ≈ **2 mrem/h = 20 µSv/h**

U.S. meters, forms, and license conditions speak rem and mR/h; CP-105 and international documents speak sieverts. Converting instantly — without sliding a decimal — is a certification-level skill.

sections.heading: Radiation Is Not Contamination
sections.body:
The public uses the words interchangeably. A radiographer never should, because the two hazards are controlled in completely different ways.

**Radiation** is energy in flight — photons or particles crossing space. When it strikes you, you are **exposed** (irradiated): energy is deposited, dose is received, and the moment the source is shielded or you step away, the dose stops. Nothing physical sticks to you. Critically, **gamma and X-rays at radiography energies cannot make you — or the part — radioactive**; inducing radioactivity takes neutrons. The weld you shot last night is not radioactive, and neither are you.

**Contamination** is radioactive **material** — actual atoms — in a place it is not wanted: dust on coveralls, particles on a guide-tube fitting, mud on a boot sole. *External* contamination sits on skin, clothing, or surfaces; *internal* contamination has been inhaled, swallowed, or absorbed through a wound. Contaminated objects keep emitting until the material is removed or decays. Contamination **spreads** — by hands, wind, water, tools, and tailgates — and once inside the body it delivers dose continuously, with no distance and no shielding left to invoke.

The campfire makes the distinction unforgettable: stand near it and you feel heat — that is **exposure**, gone when you walk away. Let embers land in your coat and you carry the fire with you — that is **contamination**, burning until you brush it off, and worse if you inhale the smoke.

sections.caption: Exposure versus contamination: an irradiated worker receives energy and carries nothing away; a contaminated worker carries radioactive material that keeps delivering dose until it is removed.

sections.heading: Sealed Sources and the Leak Test
sections.body:
Industrial radiography stays a radiation-only trade by engineering: the isotope is a **sealed source** — iridium-192 pellets or discs welded inside a stainless-steel capsule on its pigtail, qualified as *special form* radioactive material designed to survive accident conditions without releasing its contents. Intact, it emits gamma rays and nothing else.

The proof is the **leak test** required by **10 CFR 34.27**: a wipe taken at the nearest accessible point to the source, where contamination would accumulate, is analyzed at intervals **not to exceed six months**. The analysis must be capable of detecting **0.005 µCi (185 Bq)** of removable activity — and a result at or above that threshold means the source is leaking. The device is **immediately withdrawn from use** for decontamination and repair or disposal, and the leaking source is reported to the regulator.

In the field, certain events make you *presume* the seal is in question until proven otherwise: a guide tube crushed by a vehicle, a source assembly struck during a retrieval, fire or severe impact involving the device. The response is the contamination playbook — **back away and secure the area; nobody eats, drinks, or smokes nearby; touch nothing; notify the RSO** — because your survey meter measures dose rate, not loose material. Contamination is confirmed with **wipe samples counted on a sensitive laboratory instrument** and thin-window frisking, and one field clue is damning: a spot that keeps reading above background *after* the source is locked back in its shield.

figures.title: Sources of natural background dose vs. the occupational limit

interactive.title: Exposure or Contamination? Make the Call
interactive.intro: Each situation below is one or the other. Sort it into the right bucket — ask yourself: is this energy in flight, or radioactive material loose in the wrong place?

items.label: Standing 10 feet from an unshielded Ir-192 source for 30 seconds
items.why: Gamma photons deliver dose while you stand there, and the dose stops the moment the source is shielded or you move away. No material transfers to you.

items.label: Dust from a crushed source capsule settles on a radiographer's coveralls
items.why: Radioactive material is now riding on clothing — it keeps emitting until removed, and it can spread to skin, truck cabs, and break rooms.

items.label: Your film badge reads 40 mrem at the end of the month
items.why: The badge integrates dose equivalent from radiation that reached it during exposures — a record of energy received, not of any material present.

items.label: A worker inhales airborne particles near a leaking source
items.why: Inhaled radioactive material is internal contamination — it irradiates tissue continuously until it decays or the body eliminates it, and no distance or shielding can help.

items.label: An X-ray crawler irradiates a pipe weld for 90 seconds
items.why: Machine X-rays are pure energy: when the exposure ends the radiation is gone, and the weld is exactly as non-radioactive as before the shot.

items.label: A leak-test wipe of an exposure device counts 0.02 µCi of removable activity
items.why: Removable activity is contamination by definition — and 0.02 µCi is four times the 0.005 µCi limit of 10 CFR 34.27, so this source is leaking and the device comes out of service.

items.label: A survey meter at the restricted-area boundary reads 1.8 mR/h during a shot
items.why: That reading is dose rate from photons in flight. When the source is cranked back into its shield, the field collapses to background.

items.label: Mud tracked from a spill area frisks above background on a boot sole
items.why: Material that travels with the boot and keeps reading after the source is secured is contamination — and tracking it is exactly how contamination spreads beyond the original spill.

checkQuestions.question: Which single source contributes the most to the average American's natural background dose?
options: Cosmic radiation from space
Terrestrial gamma rays from soil and building materials
Radon and thoron decay products in the air we breathe
Potassium-40 and carbon-14 inside the body
checkQuestions.explanation: Per NCRP Report No. 160, inhaled radon and thoron decay products average about 2.28 mSv per year — nearly three-quarters of the ~3.1 mSv natural background total. Cosmic (~0.33 mSv), internal emitters (~0.29 mSv), and terrestrial gamma (~0.21 mSv) are each far smaller.

checkQuestions.question: The annual occupational TEDE limit of 5 rem, expressed in SI units, is:
options: 5 mSv
50 mSv
500 mSv
0.5 mSv
checkQuestions.explanation: 1 Sv = 100 rem, so 5 rem = 0.05 Sv = 50 mSv. Sliding this decimal in either direction is one of the most common — and most serious — unit errors a radiographer can make.

checkQuestions.question: What does the sievert capture that the gray does not?
options: The differing biological effectiveness of each radiation type, applied through the quality factor
The rate at which the dose is delivered rather than the total received
The amount of ionization the radiation produces in air
The activity of the source in disintegrations per second
checkQuestions.explanation: The gray is pure physics — energy deposited per kilogram. The sievert multiplies absorbed dose by a quality factor (1 for photons and beta, about 10 for neutrons, 20 for internal alpha) so that equal numbers mean equal expected biological harm.

checkQuestions.question: A radiographer finishes a night of shooting with a sealed, leak-tested Ir-192 source. His dosimeter shows 25 mrem for the shift. Which statement is correct?
options: He is slightly radioactive and should keep his distance from family until the activity decays
His coveralls are contaminated and must be surveyed and bagged before he leaves the site
Showering promptly will remove most of the 25 mrem he received
He received a radiation dose but carries no radioactive material — he is neither contaminated nor radioactive
checkQuestions.explanation: Gamma exposure deposits energy; it transfers no material and, at radiography energies, cannot induce radioactivity. Dose is not a substance — there is nothing to wash off and no hazard to anyone around him.

checkQuestions.question: A six-month leak test wipe on an exposure device shows 0.008 µCi of removable activity. The required action is:
options: None — the result is below the regulatory limit, so the device stays in service
Log the result and repeat the wipe at the next six-month interval
Treat the source as leaking: immediately withdraw the device from use and report it, since the result meets or exceeds 0.005 µCi (185 Bq)
Keep using the device as long as daily survey-meter readings around it remain normal
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.27 sets the leaking-source threshold at 0.005 µCi (185 Bq) of removable activity; 0.008 µCi exceeds it. The device comes out of service immediately for decontamination and repair or disposal — a dose-rate survey cannot clear it, because dose rate does not measure removable material.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Appendix A §8.1–8.3 (natural background radiation, the sievert, radiation vs. contamination)
10 CFR Part 20, §§20.1003, 20.1004, 20.1201 (definitions, units of radiation dose, occupational dose limits)
10 CFR Part 34, §34.27 (leak testing and replacement of sealed sources)
NCRP Report No. 160, Ionizing Radiation Exposure of the Population of the United States (2009)
